questions.question: What is the primary advantage of using a subagent for code review rather than having the main agent review its own code?
options: The subagent has access to more tools and can make edits during review
The subagent operates with fresh eyes in its own context window without the bias of the main agent
The subagent can review code faster because it doesn't need to understand the original implementation
The subagent automatically fixes all identified issues before reporting them
questions.explanation: A subagent provides an unbiased review because it operates in its own context window without the bias that the main agent develops while writing the code. This fresh perspective is valuable for catching issues the original author might overlook. The subagent should be restricted to read-only tools and should flag issues rather than edit files.

questions.question: When configuring a code-reviewer subagent, what type of tool access should it have?
options: Full access to all tools including file editing capabilities
Read-only tools to flag issues without editing files
Access only to git-related tools
No tool access; it should only provide text-based feedback
questions.explanation: A code-reviewer subagent should be restricted to read-only tools. This ensures the reviewer can identify and flag issues without making unauthorized changes to the codebase. The reviewer's role is to report problems, not to edit files.

questions.question: What does the `/commit-push-pr` skill do?
options: It creates a PR but requires manual commit and push steps
It handles commit, push, and PR creation all in one step
It only pushes code to the remote repository
It reviews code before allowing a commit
questions.explanation: The `/commit-push-pr` skill is a convenience feature that automates the entire workflow of committing changes, pushing them to the remote repository, and creating a pull request—all in a single command. This reduces friction by eliminating the need to run these steps manually.

questions.question: If you have a Slack MCP server configured with channels listed in your `CLAUDE.md`, what happens when you use the `/commit-push-pr` skill?
options: The PR link is automatically posted to your team's Slack channel
A Slack notification is sent only to the PR author
The skill requires manual approval in Slack before proceeding
The PR is created but Slack integration is disabled
questions.explanation: When a Slack MCP server is configured with channels listed in your `CLAUDE.md`, the `/commit-push-pr` skill automatically posts the PR link to your team's channel. This keeps the team informed about new PRs without requiring manual notification.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `--from-pr` command?
options: To create a new PR from an existing branch
To resume work on a PR later by picking up where you left off
To view the history of all PRs in the repository
To automatically merge a PR into the main branch
questions.explanation: The `--from-pr` command allows you to resume work on a specific PR by linking your session back to that PR. This is useful when you need to address review comments or fix a failing build. You run `claude --from-pr <PR_NUMBER>` to continue from where you left off.

questions.question: Why should a code-reviewer subagent configuration be checked into the repository?
options: To ensure the subagent has access to all repository secrets
To allow the entire team to use the same reviewer configuration
To prevent other teams from using the same reviewer
To automatically update the reviewer whenever the repository is cloned
questions.explanation: Checking the subagent configuration into the repository ensures consistency across the team. When the configuration is version-controlled, all team members use the same reviewer setup, which maintains uniform code review standards and expectations.

questions.question: When does the session linking to a PR occur automatically?
options: When you run the `--from-pr` command
When Claude creates a PR through `gh pr create`
When the PR is merged into the main branch
When a code review is requested from a team member
questions.explanation: Session linking happens automatically when Claude creates a PR using `gh pr create`. This automatic linking allows you to later resume the session using `--from-pr` if you need to make changes or address feedback.

questions.question: In a typical workflow, at what point should you ask Claude to use a subagent for code review?
options: After the PR has been merged into the main branch
Before you push a PR
During the initial code writing phase
Only when a team member requests a review
questions.explanation: The subagent review should occur before you push a PR. This allows you to catch and address issues early, before the code is submitted for team review. Catching problems at this stage reduces the number of review cycles needed.

questions.question: What is a key difference between the role of a code-reviewer subagent and the role of the main agent during development?
options: The subagent has more computational power than the main agent
The subagent flags issues without editing files, while the main agent writes and modifies code
The subagent can access external APIs while the main agent cannot
The subagent is only used for security reviews while the main agent handles all other reviews
questions.explanation: The subagent's role is fundamentally different from the main agent's role. The main agent writes and modifies code during development, while the subagent is restricted to read-only tools and serves only to identify and flag issues. This separation of concerns ensures objective, unbiased feedback.

questions.question: Consider a scenario where you've addressed review comments on a PR and need to make additional changes. Which command would you use to resume your session, and why would this be preferable to starting a new session?
options: Use `claude --new-session` to start fresh with a clear context
Use `claude --from-pr <PR_NUMBER>` to maintain context and continue from where you left off
Use `claude --review-pr <PR_NUMBER>` to automatically apply all suggested changes
Use `claude --merge-pr <PR_NUMBER>` to finalize the changes
questions.explanation: The `--from-pr` command resumes your session with the context of the PR you were working on. This is preferable to starting a new session because it preserves the context of your work, allowing Claude to understand the changes you've already made and the feedback you've received. Starting fresh would lose this valuable context.

questions.question: How would you design a code review workflow that leverages both the subagent review and the `/commit-push-pr` skill to minimize friction while maintaining code quality?
options: Use the subagent to review code, then use `/commit-push-pr` to push changes directly without addressing subagent feedback
Skip the subagent review and use `/commit-push-pr` immediately to save time
Use the subagent to review code and identify issues, address the feedback, then use `/commit-push-pr` to commit, push, and create the PR in one step
Use `/commit-push-pr` first to create the PR, then ask the subagent to review the PR after it's been pushed
questions.explanation: The optimal workflow combines both features: first use the subagent for an unbiased review before pushing, address any issues it identifies, then use `/commit-push-pr` to handle the entire commit-to-PR flow in one step. This approach catches issues early while automating the final steps, reducing friction without sacrificing quality.

questions.question: What potential challenges might arise if a code-reviewer subagent configuration is not checked into the repository, and how would this affect team collaboration?
options: Different team members might use different reviewer configurations, leading to inconsistent code review standards and making it difficult to maintain uniform quality expectations
The subagent would be unable to access the repository and would refuse to perform reviews
The repository would automatically reject all PRs created without a checked-in configuration
Team members would be unable to use the `/commit-push-pr` skill without a configuration file
questions.explanation: Without a checked-in configuration, team members might create or use different subagent setups, leading to inconsistent review standards. This inconsistency undermines code quality goals and makes it harder for the team to maintain shared expectations about what constitutes acceptable code. Checking the configuration into the repository ensures everyone uses the same reviewer setup.
